item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Eric-Paul Riege at Burke Artist Studio
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

To celebrate the opening weekend of Eric-Paul Riege: ojo|-|ólǫ́, visit the artist offsite at the Burke Artist Studio, located in the Northwest Native Art Gallery at the Burke Museum, just a few blocks from the Henry. As part of Free First Thursday at the Burke, visitors will have the chance to watch the artist at work and speak with Riege about his process. ojo|-|ólǫ́ is based on Eric-Paul Riege's material research and engagement with the Navajo collections held by the Burke Museum, as well as the Brown University's Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology. The resulting new body of work is informed by ancestral knowledge and traditions contained within Indigenous objects, and which aims, in part, to redress practices of cultural dispossession by museums and other institutions. Bio Eric-Paul Riege \(Diné\) is a weaver and fiber artist working in collage, durational performance, installation, woven sculpture, and wearable art. Using weaving as both means and metaphor to tell hybrid tales that interlace stories from Diné spirituality with his own interpretations and cosmology, he understands his artworks as animate and mobile. His practice pays homage and links him to generations of weavers in his family who aid him in generating spaces of sanctuary. Riege's recent solo exhibitions include iiZiiT \[3\]: RIEGE Jewelry \+ Supply at Canal Projects, New York \(2025\), Hammer Projects: Eric Paul Riege at the Hammer Museum, Los Angeles \(2022-2023\), and Hól ́ǫ-it xistz at the Institute of Contemporary Art Miami \(2019\). His recent group exhibitions include the 24th Biennale of Sydney in Australia \(2024\), Indian Theater: Native Performance, Art, and Self-Determination \(2023\), Prospect.5 Triennial in New Orleans \(2022\), and the Toronto Biennial of Art \(2022\). He holds a BFA in Art Studio and Ecology from the University of New Mexico in Albuquerque. His work is collected by Forge Project and ICA Miami, among others. He is represented by Bockley Gallery \(MN\) and STARS Gallery \(CA\). Riege is a member of the Charcoal Streaked Division of the Red Running Into the Water clan. He was born and is based in Na'nízhoozhí \[Gallup, New Mexico\].

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Evening hours will feature extra activities like live music, drop-in art making, and in-gallery experiences for visitors. 11:30 am, 1:30 pm, 6 pm | Public Tour: Highlights from SAM's Collection These 60-minute tours meet at the top of the 3rd floor escalators. 5:30-6:30 pm | SAM Gallery Artist Talk SAM Gallery Free First Thursday Evenings Presented by

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Scott Gibson - Visual Symphony II
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Gray Sky Gallery, 320 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

In March, Visual Symphony II Scott Gibson invites viewers into a world where music and art collide. Each work is an exploration of the vivid, sensory experience of sound translating into vibrant, emotional visuals. As an artist with synesthesia each work expresses Scott’s personal perception of what music looks like. Scott uses resin techniques that he has pioneered and pushes the boundaries of this medium.

item.title: EMBODIED ALTARS DANCE CLASS
item.description:
Monday, April 6, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center, 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA

EMBODIED ALTARS is an all-levels dance class hosted by Daybreak Star Radio. Designed by Indigenous Chicana artist and El Sueño director, Alicia Mullikin. Dancers will utilize Indigenous principles within movement, meditation, and song as a way to practice gratitude, honor the land, and recognize their ancestors. The class is for those who are interested in reflection, intention setting, and a discovery of their own stories. No dance experience necessary. Class Time: Mondays, 6:30-8PM No class 9/15/25 No class 10/13/25 Venue: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA 98199 Sliding Scale: $10-$20\* per class. Sign Up Now!   This venue is ADA accessible w/ ADA bathrooms and a wheelchair lift to the second floor. Class will be held in the ballroom space, this area has 5 ADA parking spaces. There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

Event details

item.title: Danielle Girard with Danya Kukafka - 'Pinky Swear'
item.description:
Monday, April 6, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Third Place Books Ravenna, 6504 20th Ave NE Seattle WA 98115, Seattle, WA

When a young mom's surrogate disappears days before the due date, the ensuing search uncovers dark truths and the power of a mother's love. Third Place Books welcomes bestselling author and Killer Women podcast host Danielle Girard to our Ravenna store for a conversation about her new book Pinky Swear — a thriller about motherhood, friendship, and how well we can ever really know another person. She will be joined in conversation by Danya Kukafka, author of Notes on an Execution and Girl in Snow. This event is free and open to the public. For important updates, RSVP is highly recommended in advance. This event will include a public signing and time for audience Q&A. Sustain our author series by purchasing a copy of the featured books!

item.title: Wife of Headless Man Investigates Her Own Disappearance
item.description:
Monday, April 6, 2026 at 7:30 PM

Location: Annex Theatre, 1100 E Pike St, Seattle, WA

Written by Yussef El Guindi Directed by Lucien Oberleitner A reporter experiences a chunk of missing time after interviewing a tech billionaire who hasn’t taken kindly to her negative articles about him. Was she drugged during the meal? How did she end up in a hotel room after meeting him in a restaurant? And if she was drugged, is that why, when she returns home, her husband appears as fully alive and functioning and headless?

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Tuesday, April 7, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Get your ideas out of your head and onto the page through guided writing exercises. Event details

item.title: Type Design Basics
item.description:
Tuesday, April 7, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview So you want to design a typeface? Learn how to hand-draw letters and digitize them to create your own unique functional font! In this practical workshop, students will create an original text typeface based on traditional European calligraphy. Whether you want to develop your typography skills, improve your lettering practice, or become a type designer, learning the fundamentals of type design can bring your skills to the next level. Explore both analog media \(pen on paper\) and digital media \(computer\). Learn how to use a calligraphy pen to develop an understanding of the underlying structure of the Latin alphabet. Then you can work on improving your drawings digitally, and create a typeface using Glyphs \(or the font editor of your choice\).   Class time is dedicated to working on your project with individualized feedback, supplemented by lectures, technical demonstrations, and group critique. By the end of the class, you will have a working text typeface of your own creation, as well as a sketch-to-screen workflow that you can use for future font creation. Students will be encouraged to spend up to 10 hours on homework outside of class time. Designing a typeface is a laborious process, but time flies when experimenting, drawing, and editing! This class is offered in partnership with the amazing Seattle Creative Academy \(but this class is not part of their program and is not for credit/degree\)! See also: Experimental Lettering and other Graphic Design or art classes on our website. Learning Objectives Learn the basics of calligraphy Understand the structure and patterning of the Latin alphabet Create your own original letterforms Digitize and refine your original design Learn how to optimize a typeface for text Become familiar with a sketch-to-screen digital type design workflow Who Might Enjoy This Class? Anyone who wants to develop their own custom font Graphic designers, illustrators, lettering artists, type designers -- anyone with related experience who would like to hone their type design skills Architects, engineers, artists, crafters, writers, book lovers -- anyone interested in the form of letters who would enjoy creating and using their own handmade font No prerequisites, but basic graphic design/typography knowledge is recommended

Event details

item.title: Currents & ERRA
item.description:
Tuesday, April 7, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: The Showbox, 1426 1st Ave., Seattle, WA

Showbox Presents The Silence Follows Tour 2026 CURRENTS & ERRA with Caskets and Aviana TUE, 7 APR 2026 at 06:30PM PDT Ages: All Ages to Enter, 21 & Over to Drink Doors Open: 06:00PM OnSale: Fri, 19 Dec 2025 at 10:00AM PST Announcement: Tue, 16 Dec 2025 at 07:00AM PST Currents Currents never compromise neither creatively, nor philosophically.As if controlling chaos by hand, the Connecticut quintet—Brian Wille \[vocals\], Chris Wiseman \[guitar\], RyanCastaldi \[guitar\], Matt Young \[drums\], and Christian Pulgarin \[bass\]—balance an inclination for invention withinnate intensity, defying expectations and expanding the scope of heavy music in the process. By following aninternal creative compass, the group have continued to tread a singular path without comparison. Teeteringon an axis of extreme heaviness and transfixing melody, this path reaches another peak on their 2025 EP, AllThat Follows \[SHARPTONE\].Breaking free from expectations and rules, it represents both a signature body of work and a natural stepthrough new territory.“Over the years, we didn’t listen to anyone else, we made the choices we wanted to make, and we believed inthe people who stood behind us,” Brian reflects. “Each day, it’s always just been one foot in front of the other.Since nobody ever thought this would be a viable career option, it’s so meaningful that people want musicfrom us.”Long before tallying hundreds of millions of streams and playing to sold-out crowds, the spark behindCurrents began to flicker as early as 2011. Brian had joined the fold during 2015, and the band pushedforward with a series of critically acclaimed fan favorite releases such as The Place I Feel Safest \[2017\], I LetThe Devil In EP \[2018\], The Way It Ends \[2020\], and The Death We Seek \[2023\]. Following the latter’s release,Everything Is Noise raved, “Currents are one of the few that are capable of crafting a modern metalcore soundthat is unrelenting in every which way whilst still being incredibly accessible.” Beyond praise courtesy ofMetalSucks and more, Distorted Sound rated it “9-out-of-10,” proceeding to profess, “It makes the future ofthis brilliant set of musicians one of the most exciting prospects in music to this day.” Along the way, theyshared stages with everyone from Parkway Drive and Beartooth to Ice Nine Kills, We Came As Romans, andMiss May I.In between non-stop touring, All That Follows came together. Once again, they worked with producer RyanLeitru \[We Came As Romans, Wolves At The Gate\] on a handful of tracks, while Brian and Chris co-producedthe rest of the material.“For the EP, we really compiled some of our strongest songs that made sense together,” he goes on.Currents teased this era with “It Only Gets Darker,” which instantly reeled in over 1.9 million Spotify streams.Among various highlights, “My Severance” evinces the band’s penchant for dynamics. Icy beeps pulsate acrossa violent chugging groove punctured by an ethereal arpeggio and glimmering harmonics. It climaxes on acathartic clean chorus, “Locked in isolation, thrown away. This is my severance.” “It discusses how we’re taken advantage of by people who think they know better because they have moremoney than God and make whatever decisions they want,” he reveals. “They gain control of the governmentby lobbying. We have no way of combatting it unless we’re all together. So, you renounce your faith in theinstitution and stand up to it.”“Making Circles” slips from a wave of guttural distortion into the undertow of fragile verses. The tensionmoves cyclically in a celestial rhythm as Brian sets the scene, “Making circles in the dark to find we’re clouded;we’re lost. No use counting on the stars to guide us over it, this constant motion.”On the other end of the spectrum, “Can’t Turn Back” finds Currents in a crushing pocket. Guttural growls grateup against screeching guitar and a rumbling groove. Vitriol practically seeps through Brian’s words on hisscreams, “Hit the floor. What the fuck do you take me for?” All hell breaks loose, summoning a proclamationright from the gut, “Bow your head to this kingdom.”“It’s a betrayal song,” he sighs. “It’s directed at a person who believes he’s on top of the world, but he doesn’trealize he’s sowing his own fate.”The finale “Rise & Fall” revolves around a riff call-and-response, losing ground in a torrent of propulsivedrums. The hook holds onto the vestiges of a crumbling institution codified in the refrain, “I can’t find what’sleft for me besides another rise and fall. It took all that’s left of me and doubt is still in the way.” Emotionseethes on the bridge until a final bludgeoning breakdown.“It ends with a bunch of questions,” he states. “Is this going to happen to us? What becomes of this? We’verenounced our faith, but how does it end? Is there a second coming, the apocalypse, the singularity, or theafterlife? You don’t know how it’s going to shake out. There is only the rise and fall.”However, Currents are still in the midst of their own quiet rise, and it’s not stopping.“For the most part, I take the worst things I can think of and put them in the music,” he concludes. “I pour alot of hatred, sadness, frustration, confusion, and betrayal into Currents. What comes out is the opposite ofwhat goes in though. When we play a show, people lose their minds, and they’re smiling. We’re trying toprovide a healthy outlet.” ERRA Hailing from Birmingham, Alabama - ERRA has continuously brought titanic riffs, enchanting melodies, and immersive soundscapes to each of their progressive metal albums. They have been described as part mind-altering substance, part meditation, reveling in each musical moment of exultation. ERRA regularly appears on tastemaker playlists like Kickass Metal on Spotify and has charted multiple times on the Billboard’s Heatseekers Albums at No. 1. Most recently ERRA’s sixth album, CURE, strengthens and deepens the forward-thinking progressive metalcore they built their reputation on. It’s an album full of atmospheric ecstasy and moody groove on the heels of their stunning 2021 self-titled set. CURE charts an ambitious course for ERRA’s next era. They’ve toured extensively, with bands including Bad Omens, TesseracT, Ice Nine Kills, Dance Gavin Dance, August Burns Red, and Wage War and continually headline their own sold out tours all across the world.

Event details

item.title: Chuckle Bucket Tuesday @ Club Comedy Seattle (Open Mic)
item.description:
Tuesday, April 7, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Club Comedy Seattle, 328 15th Ave E, Seattle, WA

Club Comedy Seattle hosts Chuckle Bucket Tuesday each week. The luck of the bucket rules the night. Comics drop their name in and hope to be drawn for one of the spots on the list to tell jokes. It's fun, fast-paced and will keep you laughing hard on your Tuesday night. Door 7:00PM/Show 7:30PM Admission: $7 \+ $0.35 City Of Seattle Admission Tax 21\+ With Valid ID REQUIRED for entry. NO REFUNDS. FYI: If you have a large party it's easier for us to seat you all together if you purchase all the tickets under one name. . Club Comedy Seattle FAQ Is there an age/ID requirement? Yes. We are strictly 21\+ and valid physical ID is REQUIRED For Entry Is Seating First Come/First Served? Yes. The room is all two top tables and we seat guests in the order in which they arrive. Can I reserve a table for a group? We do not take reservations for tables, but groups can arrive early to secure seating together. Are tickets available at the door if an event is sold out online? If an event is sold out online there are no tickets available at the door. What are the club’s hours of operation? The club is open Tuesday to Sunday. Doors open a half hour before each show. What is the Parking like? Generally there is street parking available in the blocks surrounding the club. There are also affordable parking lots on 16th Ave E right around the block from the club along with other easy options in the neighborhood Is there a dress code? There is no formal dress code, but we recommend smart casual attire. Can I bring food or drinks? Outside food and drinks are not allowed. We have a full bar and a menu available for your enjoyment. What if I can't make it and need to cancel my ticket? All ticket sales are final. However, if you can’t make it, please contact us through Eventbrite in advance, and we’ll do our best to accommodate you. Will there be an opener for the headliner? Yes, headliner shows feature a lineup of comedians, including a host, an opener, and the headliner. Will the comics talk to me like I see in online videos all the time? Most likely they won't. Crowd work videos are popular for posting online, but crowd work doesn't generally happen at the online frequency in live comedy show situations. Some comics do engage the crowd as part of their regular set and it's generally always fun and more about making fun of themselves than making fun of the crowd. How can I get on the Cheap Laughs Open Mic On Wednesdays? Submissions for spots on the Wednesday Cheap Laughs Open Mic are accepted only on the Tuesday before between 12:00AM and 11:59PM via email at openmic@clubcomedyseattle.com Do you offer group discounts? Yes, we offer discounts for groups of ten or more. Please contact us for more details. Can I meet the comedians after the show? Meet-and-greet opportunities vary by event. Please ask staff for details after the show.

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, April 8, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.description:
Wednesday, April 8, 2026 at 10 AM

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, April 8, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, April 8, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Wednesday, April 8, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Mary Jane
item.description:
Wednesday, April 8, 2026 at 2 PM

Location: Leo K. Theater, 155 Mercer St., Seattle, WA

Mary Jane is a cheerful caregiver and unflagging advocate for her toddler, Alex, who lives with cerebral palsy and chronic illness—but the American healthcare system can wear anybody down, especially a single parent. While navigating her son’s health challenges, Mary Jane meets and builds community with women from all walks of life, experiencing joy and connection amidst the distress and heartbreak. Poignantly humorous and deeply cathartic, this semi-autobiographical drama by Tony-nominated playwright Amy Herzog is a love letter to caregivers and support systems of all kinds. By Amy Herzog Directed by Allison Narver

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: SP 101: Intro to Vera's Screen Print Studio
item.description:
Wednesday, April 8, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Book-It Repertory Theatre, 305 Harrison Street, Seattle, WA

This 2-hour, comprehensive hands-on class teaches you all the basics of screen printing at VERA’s screen printing studio: From hand-drawing a stencil on transparency, coating and burning a screen, to pulling your very first print! Get comfortable with Vera's shop layout, equipment, and culture. Learn techniques for creating screens, terminology, and more to create 1-color projects in the print shop. Students will walk away with the basic skills for screen printing 1-color paper prints, and their own print of a special Vera design, specific to the 101 class. This class is a prerequisite for 200-level classes at VERA, as well as access to Open Sessions to work on your own projects in VERA’s screen printing studio.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Silent Book Club Night!!
item.description:
Wednesday, April 8, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Quinn's Pub, 1001 East Pike Street, Seattle, WA

Welcome to Capitol Hill's Silent Book Club! We meet twice a month at a different restaurant or coffee shop around Capitol Hill to show support to our wonderful community! We aren't your typical book club. There is no assigned reading and you're welcome to use any medium to enjoy your story \(i.e. paperback, audiobook, kindle, etc.\). This is a judgement free zone. If you're into fiction, non-fiction, or graphic novels, bring whatever you enjoy! Our mission is to create a welcoming environment for a diverse group to share their love of books while supporting local businesses in the process. Whether you're a bookworm or just looking for a relaxing night out, this event is perfect for anyone who wants to be a part of our bookish community. How it works: 6:30 PM - Get settled, order food/drinks, meet fellow book lovers 7:00 PM - Read whatever book you like in your preferred platform 8:00 PM - Socialize, keep reading, or pay your bill and enjoy the rest of your night 9:00 PM - Quinn's Pub closes Come alone or bring a friend. Don't miss out on this unique experience! Follow us on Instagram to stay up to date on our upcoming events: @silentbookclubcapitolhill. \*\*\*This is a free event but to show our appreciation to Quinn's, a purchase is required.

item.title: Scott Gibson - Visual Symphony II

Thursday, April 9, 2026 at 12 PM

In March, Visual Symphony II Scott Gibson invites viewers into a world where music and art collide. Each work is an exploration of the vivid, sensory experience of sound translating into vibrant, emotional visuals. item.title: Contemporary Dance for Every Body
item.description:
Thursday, April 9, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview Would you like to cultivate body awareness through movement? Explore a grounded and natural approach to moving your body, led by a contemporary dance instructor. Through floorwork, movement sequences, and phrasing, this class cultivates body awareness. Explore efficiency in movement transitions. Develop a deeper understanding of how your body moves naturally in relation to the floor, emphasizing organic flow, continuity, spirality, softness, core engagement, and the intelligent use of gravity. Open your mind and body through guided movement! Explore points of support, releasing tension, and engaging the core to facilitate flow and prevent injury. Movement principles will draw from contemporary dance and somatic practices, encouraging dancers to move with clarity, sensitivity, and presence. This class is for dancers, actors, physical theater artists, yoga/movement practitioners, and anyone interested in exploring movement in their bodies -- no experience necessary. Designed to be inclusive, this class offers both accessibility and challenge to movers at various levels and all backgrounds.  Learning Objectives Understand and embody key principles of movement floorwork, including gravity, spirality, and core initiation. Improve movement continuity and flow between levels \(floor to standing and vice versa\). Explore softness and efficiency through core engagement. Identify and use anatomical points of support for safe and fluid movement. Develop new dance tools Feel more connected with the possibilities of your body in a conscious way to live a more present, healthy, and aware life. Who Might Enjoy This Class? Adult learners of all backgrounds \(18\+\), including dancers, actors, physical theater artists, yoga/movement practitioners Anyone interested in embodied movement practices. Some prior movement or dance experience is helpful but not required. The class will be structured to accommodate varying levels of experience and physical ability.

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Friday, April 10, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Friday, April 10, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Friday, April 10, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Friday, April 10, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Friday, April 10, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

Friday, April 10, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Gray Sky Gallery, 320 First Ave. S., Seattle, WA

In March, Visual Symphony II Scott Gibson invites viewers into a world where music and art collide. Each work is an exploration of the vivid, sensory experience of sound translating into vibrant, emotional visuals. As an artist with synesthesia each work expresses Scott’s personal perception of what music looks like. Scott uses resin techniques that he has pioneered and pushes the boundaries of this medium.

Event details

item.title: Screen Printing Open Session
item.description:
Friday, April 10, 2026 at 2 PM

Location: Book-It Repertory Theatre, 305 Harrison Street, Seattle, WA

You MUST take the SP 101: Intro to Vera's Screen Print Studio class before you can sign up for an Open Session\\\*\*\* Vera’s screen print studio offers four-hour Open Sessions where individuals who have completed the introductory class may use our screen print lab to work on their own projects. Our Open Sessions are looked over by a volunteer moderator, who can provide you with advice and guidance for anything taught in our Screen Printing 101 class. We offer three different kinds of tickets at Open Sessions: Vacuum Table for paper and other flat media, T-shirt Press for shirts, tote bags and other fabrics, and Screen prep for just screen preparation. Each ticket is limited to two. If the ticket you are looking for is sold out, please add yourself to the waitlist so you can be notified when a ticket becomes available. For Open Sessions, Vera provides: - Rentable screens - Emulsion and emulsion remover - Squegees - Basic tools like spatulas, rulers, tape, etc. Participants must provide their own: - Substrate \(paper, t-shirts, etc\) - Water based ink Vera \*does not\* allow the use of non-water based inks at our studio, as our studio is not set up to safely process non-water based ink. If you have any questions, please email matt@theveraproject.org. \*\*FAQ\*\* 1. Can Vera print a transparent stencil for me? Yes — You must send us your stencil at least 24 hours before the start of your session. Your file must be a .PDF, no larger than 12" x 18", and ready to print. Vera cannot make any edits to your file. Stencils are $5 each and can be paid via Debit/Credit when you arrive. If you are not interested in getting a stencil printed at Vera, you can print your own on transparency at a FedEx or similar print service location. 2. What if I need direct assistance on my projects? Our moderators are trained to assist with any topics covered in the SP 101 class offered at Vera. While some may be able to give you basic advice on your project, they are not expected to ask as a direct instructor. If you have a project where you would like one-on-one guidance, please reach out to matt@theveraproject.org to request private tutoring. 3. I really want to use your studio during an Open Session, but there are no slots available? Our studio is in high demand, so we apologize if our Open Session is sold out — We have limited space in the studio and several events every day at Vera, so it can be difficult for us to find more time for Open Sessions. If you're interested in booking the studio outside of Open Sessions, please email matt@theveraproject.org to discuss private rental rates and availability.

item.title: Live Music Happy Hour Joe Casalini Trio
item.description:
Friday, April 10, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: The Royal Room, 5000 Rainier Ave South, Seattle, WA

Join us for Live Music Happy Hour with the Joe Casalini Trio. The Casalini Trio has played happy hours at the Royal Room since 2019. They play laid-back, swinging versions of great tunes by Duke Ellington, Billy Strayhorn, Hoagie Carmichael, Phillip Johnston, and other great American songwriters of the last 100 years. The happy hour usually lasts about an hour and a half. Good drinks, good people, good ambience, and there’s never a cover charge, although the band often encourages donations to local food banks and other worthy causes.

item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Learn the basics of hand embroidery in this beginner-friendly youth workshop inspired by classic Nordic floral motifs. Students will practice simple stitches while creating designs based on leaves, flowers, and natural patterns found in Nordic landscapes. No experience necessary; all materials are provided. This hands-on program offers a calm, creative space to slow down, try something new, and leave with a finished piece and skills to continue stitching at home.

item.title: Youth Soapstone Carving Workshops At Best of the Northwest Art Show
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10:30 AM

Location: Magnuson Park Hangar 30, 6310 NE 74th St., Seattle, WA

The Northwest Stone Sculptors Association \(NWSSA\) is excited to offer Youth Soapstone Carving Workshops during the Northwest Art Alliance Best of the Northwest Spring Show on April 11th & 12th at Magnuson Park Hangar 30. Youth can participate an up-close, hands-on experience with creativity by learning to carve their own soapstone animal figurine. Artist instructors from the Northwest Stone Sculptors Association will guide youth participants Ages 7 – 17 through the process of shaping this beautiful, natural material into a figurine to take home. These workshops are designed to promote fine motor skills, 3D spatial reasoning, and artistic confidence. The soapstone and all necessary kid-safe tools and water to minimize dust will be provided. The youth workshops are Free with admission to the Best of the Northwest Show.   Two 90 minute sessions are available, on Saturday April 11th and Sunday, April 12th beginning at 10:30 am and 1:30 pm.  Spots are limited and registration is encouraged.

Event details

item.title: Saturdays at SAM
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Create and connect every first, second, and third Saturday. This lively space is filled with hands-on art making, concerts, and moments of community-all inspired by SAM's permanent collections and exhibitions. Whether you're an art enthusiast, a curious newcomer, or just looking for a place to recharge this weekend, you're always welcome. Learn about the artwork through interactive activities, meet new friends, or simply enjoy the atmosphere before exploring the galleries. Programs in SAM's entry gathering spaces are always free and open to everyone. Food and beverages from Shooby Doo Catering will be available for purchase in theBrotman Forum. Bring your curiosity and imagination, we'll provide the rest. 11 am-2 pm | Jazz with Beserat Tafesse South Hall Trombonist Beserat Tafesse and his band of cutting-edge musicians define the present-day sound of Jazz in Seattle.Born to Ethiopian immigrants in the Pacific Northwest, Beserat Tafesse grew up with a wide array of musical and cultural influences. After cementing his love for Jazz, he attended the Manhattan School of Music, studying Jazz Trombone with some of the greats, and shedding nightly with many of New York's living legends. Now making Seattle his home once again, Beserat is at the center of the Jazz community's most inspired music.

item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 11 AM

Play, create, and connect in the Brotman Forum every first, second, and third Saturday, 11 am–2 pm. This lively space is filled with hands-on art making, live music, and moments of community—all inspired by SAM’s permanent collections and exhibitions. Whether you’re an art enthusiast, a curious newcomer, or just looking for a place to recharge this weekend, you’re always welcome. Learn about the artwork through interactive activities, meet new friends, or simply enjoy the atmosphere before exploring the galleries. Programs in SAM’s gathering spaces in the museum’s entry area are always free and open to everyone. Bring your curiosity and imagination, we’ll provide the rest.

item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Get ready to celebrate Renton’s rooftop dragon at the 7th Annual Dragon’s Landing Street Fair and Dragon Egg Treasure Hunt! 1️⃣ Dragon Egg Scavenger Hunt Starting April 10, follow the clues, find hidden dragon eggs throughout the city, and log your discoveries for a chance to win amazing dragon treasure! 2️⃣ Student Poster Contest K–12 students are invited to design the official poster for the 7th Annual Dragon’s Landing Event. Winners will have their artwork featured at the event, in the student art show, and online! Submissions due March 6 3️⃣ Window Decorating Contest for Businesses Local businesses are invited to decorate their windows in dragon-inspired creativity! Show off your storefront, engage the community, and add to the festive magic of downtown Renton. ✨ Event Day – Sunday, April 12, 2026 Enjoy captivating performances, creative crafts, and fun for all ages as we come together to celebrate our rooftop dragon! Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Youth can participate an up-close, hands-on experience with creativity by learning to carve their own soapstone animal figurine. Artist instructors from the Northwest Stone Sculptors Association will guide youth participants Ages 7 – 17 through the process of shaping this beautiful, natural material into a figurine to take home. item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.description:

Directed by Jeannette LeGault, music direction by Aimee Hong , and intimacy and fight choreography by Lee Ann Hittenberger.  Performances run April 3-18, 2026, at Theatre Off Jackson \(409 7th Ave. S. Seattle 98104\) Told through a feminist lens, and performed by an all women/TGNC ensemble, our production of this classic musical explores the ongoing fight for women’s rights, including the pivotal battles of the 21st century. Both urgent and hopeful, this powerful reimagining asks, “What does it mean to dream the impossible dream when your voice, your rights, and your choices are at risk?” Written by Dale Wasserman; Music by Mitch Leigh; Lyrics by Joe Darion; Original production directed by Albert Marre. item.title: Gabor Maté
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7:30 PM

Location: McCaw Hall, 321 Mercer Street, Seattle, WA

Embark on a profound journey into the intricate landscapes of the human mind and soul with renowned physician, speaker, and bestselling author, Dr. Gabor Maté. With compassion and unwavering authenticity, Dr. Maté explores the interconnectedness of mind, body, and spirit, shedding light on the underlying causes of human suffering and resilience. Drawing from his groundbreaking research on addiction, trauma, and the impact of early childhood experiences, he offers a transformative perspective on healing and personal growth. Through compelling anecdotes, scientific research, and practical wisdom, Dr. Maté invites audiences to reconsider their understanding of mental health, addiction, and the nature of human connection. Whether you're a healthcare professional, a mental health advocate, or simply a curious seeker of truth, this event promises to inspire, educate, and ignite a deeper understanding of the human condition.

item.title: Type Design Basics
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview So you want to design a typeface? Learn how to hand-draw letters and digitize them to create your own unique functional font! In this practical workshop, students will create an original text typeface based on traditional European calligraphy. Whether you want to develop your typography skills, improve your lettering practice, or become a type designer, learning the fundamentals of type design can bring your skills to the next level. Explore both analog media \(pen on paper\) and digital media \(computer\). Learn how to use a calligraphy pen to develop an understanding of the underlying structure of the Latin alphabet. Then you can work on improving your drawings digitally, and create a typeface using Glyphs \(or the font editor of your choice\).   Class time is dedicated to working on your project with individualized feedback, supplemented by lectures, technical demonstrations, and group critique. By the end of the class, you will have a working text typeface of your own creation, as well as a sketch-to-screen workflow that you can use for future font creation. Students will be encouraged to spend up to 10 hours on homework outside of class time. Designing a typeface is a laborious process, but time flies when experimenting, drawing, and editing! This class is offered in partnership with the amazing Seattle Creative Academy \(but this class is not part of their program and is not for credit/degree\)! See also: Experimental Lettering and other Graphic Design or art classes on our website. Learning Objectives Learn the basics of calligraphy Understand the structure and patterning of the Latin alphabet Create your own original letterforms Digitize and refine your original design Learn how to optimize a typeface for text Become familiar with a sketch-to-screen digital type design workflow Who Might Enjoy This Class? Anyone who wants to develop their own custom font Graphic designers, illustrators, lettering artists, type designers -- anyone with related experience who would like to hone their type design skills Architects, engineers, artists, crafters, writers, book lovers -- anyone interested in the form of letters who would enjoy creating and using their own handmade font No prerequisites, but basic graphic design/typography knowledge is recommended

item.title: Write a Feature Film
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview Do you want to write a screenplay for a feature film, but don’t know where to start? Perhaps you’re a beginner curious about the mechanics of screenplays, or a longtime writer feeling a bit stuck, or a director who would enjoy a new perspective. In this class, gain the skills to turn your ideas into a solid screenplay for a feature film. Gain the tools and building blocks to help guide each part of the writing process for a feature-length screenplay. Learn how to be successful with each step, from idea generation, to writing and revising, and especially outlining and structuring your film. Each 90-minute class will include one or more short in-class exercises, from brainstorming to writing and revising scenes. In the sixth and final class, you will have an opportunity to read an excerpt of your script or present a detailed synopsis to classmates. You’ll be encouraged to work between classes. We’ll look at ways to organize your ideas into an outline that can advance progressively closer to a feature film, discussing diverse and alternative approaches in your structure that might help your script connect with audiences without using clichés. Explore how three-dimensional characters drive plot and how setting, staging, and dialogue can be used to tell your story in an energetic, immersive way. We’ll discuss the differences between writing a screenplay you plan to direct yourself versus one you’re writing for someone else.  There will be handouts and/or reading suggestions offered in class. We’ll look at excerpts from books such as Aristotle’s Poetics and Susan Sontag’s Against Interpretation, as well as film clips and interviews with a diverse array of famous filmmakers across time and space. Get support and guidance on how to find inspiration for your own personal artistic expression and the drive to finish your project. Explore the many joys that can be found in this unique, creative, and multifaceted art form. Learning Objectives Brainstorm and organize ideas for a screenplay Approach structure in a flexible way that allows for different types of films Create three-dimensional characters that form the basis of a story How form can be progressive and new, just like content Find your voice and learn to write comfortably and confidently as yourself Who Might Enjoy This Class? Writers interested in exploring how they might approach a screenplay Anyone who wants to develop their voice as a screenwriter and filmmaker People who have begun a feature-length screenplay and would like the support to continue Artists drawn to other parts of the filmmaking process who would like to explore the writing side Anyone interested in how films are structured and what screenplays are made of Filmmakers who want to create an unconventional piece without sacrificing character and structure No experience is needed; everyone is welcome!

item.title: Chuckle Bucket Tuesday @ Club Comedy Seattle (Open Mic)
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Club Comedy Seattle, 328 15th Ave E, Seattle, WA

Club Comedy Seattle hosts Chuckle Bucket Tuesday each week. The luck of the bucket rules the night. Comics drop their name in and hope to be drawn for one of the spots on the list to tell jokes. It's fun, fast-paced and will keep you laughing hard on your Tuesday night. Door 7:00PM/Show 7:30PM Admission: $7 \+ $0.35 City Of Seattle Admission Tax 21\+ With Valid ID REQUIRED for entry. NO REFUNDS. FYI: If you have a large party it's easier for us to seat you all together if you purchase all the tickets under one name. . Club Comedy Seattle FAQ Is there an age/ID requirement? Yes. We are strictly 21\+ and valid physical ID is REQUIRED For Entry Is Seating First Come/First Served? Yes. The room is all two top tables and we seat guests in the order in which they arrive. Can I reserve a table for a group? We do not take reservations for tables, but groups can arrive early to secure seating together. Are tickets available at the door if an event is sold out online? If an event is sold out online there are no tickets available at the door. What are the club’s hours of operation? The club is open Tuesday to Sunday. Doors open a half hour before each show. What is the Parking like? Generally there is street parking available in the blocks surrounding the club. There are also affordable parking lots on 16th Ave E right around the block from the club along with other easy options in the neighborhood Is there a dress code? There is no formal dress code, but we recommend smart casual attire. Can I bring food or drinks? Outside food and drinks are not allowed. We have a full bar and a menu available for your enjoyment. What if I can't make it and need to cancel my ticket? All ticket sales are final. However, if you can’t make it, please contact us through Eventbrite in advance, and we’ll do our best to accommodate you. Will there be an opener for the headliner? Yes, headliner shows feature a lineup of comedians, including a host, an opener, and the headliner. Will the comics talk to me like I see in online videos all the time? Most likely they won't. Crowd work videos are popular for posting online, but crowd work doesn't generally happen at the online frequency in live comedy show situations. Some comics do engage the crowd as part of their regular set and it's generally always fun and more about making fun of themselves than making fun of the crowd. How can I get on the Cheap Laughs Open Mic On Wednesdays? Submissions for spots on the Wednesday Cheap Laughs Open Mic are accepted only on the Tuesday before between 12:00AM and 11:59PM via email at openmic@clubcomedyseattle.com Do you offer group discounts? Yes, we offer discounts for groups of ten or more. Please contact us for more details. Can I meet the comedians after the show? Meet-and-greet opportunities vary by event. Please ask staff for details after the show.

item.title: Hasan Raheem
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Neumos, 925 E Pike St, Seattle, WA

Neumos Presents HASAN RAHEEM with Special Guests! TUE, 14 APR 2026 at 07:00PM PDT Ages: All Ages to Enter, 21 & Over to Drink Doors Open: 07:00PM OnSale: Fri, 30 Jan 2026 at 10:00AM PST Announcement: Mon, 26 Jan 2026 at 07:00AM PST Hey, I’m Hasan. I grew up in Gilgit, surrounded by mountains and quiet moments. Eventually, life brought me to Karachi, where I thought I'd just study medicine and figure things out as I went. Honestly, I didn't plan on any of this—music was just something I loved doing after class, a way to unwind, write down feelings, and jam with friends. Fast forward a bit, and now I'm touring the world, playing shows from the US to the UK, and finding myself in places I never imagined. It's genuinely been wild seeing people connect to my songs—something that started out as a personal outlet becoming something bigger, shared, and meaningful. But no matter how many stages I step onto or cities I visit, I'm still that same guy who scribbles lyrics in notebooks, stays up late chasing new melodies, and just tries to keep things real. My sound is a mix of everything—R&B, hip-hop, indie pop—because that's what feels true to me. I love experimenting with different vibes; some days I'll drop something chill, other days I'll create a track that you can't help but dance to. At the end of the day, music for me is about connection—connecting to you, connecting to myself, and telling stories that matter. It's cool knowing we're all experiencing life through music together, each song becoming part of our shared story. Important Information: \* This event is all ages. Valid ID is required to enter bar areas. \* Doors open at the listed event time. \* Event start time is generally one hour after doors open but is subject to change without notice. \* All tickets are nonrefundable with the exception of event cancellation. \* Support acts are subject to change. \* Orders in violation of the published ticket limit are subject to cancellation without notice.

item.title: Oklou
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Showbox SoDo, 1700 1st Ave. South, Seattle, WA

French vocalist, producer and composer Oklou returned with "family and friends" in September 2024, the first solo single since her highly celebrated full-length mixtape galore. The new song is an ethereal, haunting reflection on the mystery of life, with production that features arpeggiated marimbas, lush choral textures and intricate sound design. "Are you even real?" she asks in her cryptic lyrics. The single arrives alongside a music video by director Gil Gharbi that shows Oklou and her friends running through desolate suburban streets as the camera darts searchingly. Describing the track, Oklou shares, "'family and friends' feels like a battle almost. where I'd be searching for answers to unstated questions, escaping from something intangible, invisible. A memory? Maybe I like the idea that I'd be running away from something, and I'm looking for good reasons to run. Maybe I just like that." Born Marylou Mayniel in the countryside of Western France, Oklou's first musical experiences consisted of classical piano and cello lessons before she fell in love with electronic music and took up production. Mayniel eventually moved to Paris, where she hosted weekly radio shows and performed her first live shows as Oklou. After two EPs, 2017's For The Beasts and 2018's The Rite Of May, Mayniel received international acclaim, accelerated through collaborations with Mura Masa, A. G. Cook and EASYFUN \(the last two both producers of Charli XCX's Brat\) and culminating in her full-length mixtape galore in 2020. It was a turning point in her artistic life, heralded as an innovative and introspective work by fans and media, with Dazed praising as "sophisticated in its composition yet totally cutting edge in its sound." International touring followed, including support tours with Oneohtrix Point Never and Caroline Polachek.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

From SEAtoday - Arts & Culture

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

item.title: SP 101: Intro to Vera's Screen Print Studio
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Book-It Repertory Theatre, 305 Harrison Street, Seattle, WA

This 2-hour, comprehensive hands-on class teaches you all the basics of screen printing at VERA’s screen printing studio: From hand-drawing a stencil on transparency, coating and burning a screen, to pulling your very first print! Get comfortable with Vera's shop layout, equipment, and culture. Learn techniques for creating screens, terminology, and more to create 1-color projects in the print shop. Students will walk away with the basic skills for screen printing 1-color paper prints, and their own print of a special Vera design, specific to the 101 class. This class is a prerequisite for 200-level classes at VERA, as well as access to Open Sessions to work on your own projects in VERA’s screen printing studio.

item.title: Journey
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Spokane Arena, Seattle, WA

JOURNEY is one of the most popular American rock bands of all time, creating some of the best-known songs in modern music. They have continued to tour and record into the 21st century, firing on all cylinders and making sure their fans will never stop believin'. Since the group's formation in 1973, the band has earned 19 Top 40 singles and 25 Gold and Platinum albums. The Greatest Hits album is certified 15 times-Platinum, bringing Journey into the elite club of Diamond-certified holders.

item.title: Wednesday Cheap Laughs Open Mic @ Club Comedy Seattle
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Club Comedy Seattle, 328 15th Ave E, Seattle, WA

Club Comedy Seattle hosts our Weekly Stand-Up Comedy Open Mic Every Wednesday. Door 7:30PM/Show 8:00PM. Come see how the jokes are made. Admission for the Open Mic is just $7 \+ $0.35 City Of Seattle Admission Tax 21\+ With Valid ID REQUIRED for entry. NO REFUNDS. Club Comedy Seattle FAQ Is there an age/ID requirement? Yes. We are strictly 21\+ and valid physical ID is REQUIRED For Entry Is Seating First Come/First Served? Yes. The room is all two top tables and we seat guests in the order in which they arrive. Can I reserve a table for a group? We do not take reservations for tables, but groups can arrive early to secure seating together. Are tickets available at the door if an event is sold out online? If an event is sold out online there are no tickets available at the door. What are the club’s hours of operation? The club is open Tuesday to Sunday. Doors open a half hour before each show. What is the Parking like? Generally there is street parking available in the blocks surrounding the club. There are also affordable parking lots on 16th Ave E right around the block from the club along with other easy options in the neighborhood Is there a dress code? There is no formal dress code, but we recommend smart casual attire. Can I bring food or drinks? Outside food and drinks are not allowed. We have a full bar and a menu available for your enjoyment. What if I can't make it and need to cancel my ticket? All ticket sales are final. However, if you can’t make it, please contact us through Eventbrite in advance, and we’ll do our best to accommodate you. Will there be an opener for the headliner? Yes, headliner shows feature a lineup of comedians, including a host, an opener, and the headliner. Will the comics talk to me like I see in online videos all the time? Most likely they won't. Crowd work videos are popular for posting online, but crowd work doesn't generally happen at the online frequency in live comedy show situations. Some comics do engage the crowd as part of their regular set and it's generally always fun and more about making fun of themselves than making fun of the crowd. How can I get on the Cheap Laughs Open Mic On Wednesdays? Submissions for spots on the Wednesday Cheap Laughs Open Mic are accepted only on the Tuesday before between 12:00AM and 11:59PM via email at openmic@clubcomedyseattle.com Do you offer group discounts? Yes, we offer discounts for groups of ten or more. Please contact us for more details. Can I meet the comedians after the show? Meet-and-greet opportunities vary by event. Please ask staff for details after the show.
